segments.title: Treaty limits did not prevent naval preparation
segments.summary: The opening distinction is between a treaty constraint and a government's choice not to invest in yards, personnel, and future expansion capacity.
segments.body: Clarke argues that the Washington system limited particular ships, not the ability to prepare for future growth. Escalator clauses and a possible treaty breakdown gave governments reason to retain industrial capacity, mobilization plans, and infrastructure. His central criticism is therefore often directed at spending choices rather than at the treaty text alone.

evidence.note: The lecture separates treaty restrictions from choices about infrastructure and spending.

segments.title: Why destroyers were not the central treaty failure
segments.summary: Post-First World War destroyer surpluses, rather than Washington limits, explain why many navies delayed new destroyer programs.
segments.body: The treaty left substantial room for destroyers before the London agreements, including freedom to develop a larger standard type. Clarke treats the lack of new construction as understandable because fleets already possessed many wartime destroyers and needed time to absorb operational lessons. This provides a contrast with capabilities that governments could have developed cheaply but neglected.

evidence.note: Washington restrictions and the wartime destroyer surplus are discussed together.

segments.title: Empire submarine flotillas as regional deterrence
segments.summary: A larger network of Dominion submarine flotillas could have complicated Japanese operations around Singapore and Malaya.
segments.body: Clarke notes that the Washington Treaty did not cap submarine numbers and imagines regional flotillas in Australia, India, Canada, South Africa, and New Zealand. Even relatively modest coastal boats could impose area denial and make amphibious landings or shipping movements more difficult. He presents this as a missed opportunity, while acknowledging technological, fiscal, and personnel objections.

evidence.note: The proposed Dominion submarine forces and their Singapore deterrent role are outlined.

segments.title: Canadian sloops as a practical naval foundation
segments.summary: Small sloops could have paired Canadian shipbuilding and personnel development with escort, mine warfare, and oceanographic duties.
segments.body: The lecture presents sloops as versatile, comparatively affordable vessels that could sustain a domestic industrial rhythm without the crewing burden of major warships. Mine laying, minesweeping, anti-submarine warfare, convoy escort, and survey work all make them useful beyond a single emergency. Clarke contrasts this attainable path with the much harder political task of funding an Empire-wide cruiser force.

evidence.note: Canada's possible sloop force, missions, and industrial benefits are discussed.

segments.title: The case for completing Francesco Caracciolo
segments.summary: Completing one Francesco Caracciolo-class battleship could have given Italy a stronger interwar baseline for Mediterranean naval development.
segments.body: Clarke argues that Italy should have pressed for permission to complete one of its new battleships within Washington displacement limits. In his view, the ship would have strengthened the fleet in the 1920s and 1930s and offered a more capable reference point for later Littorio-class design choices. He labels this a mistake because the option was not pursued.

evidence.note: The argument for completing Francesco Caracciolo and its design consequences is made.

segments.title: Italy's carrier and escort-sonar opportunities
segments.summary: The Italian Navy had room to pursue carrier aviation and more numerous sonar-equipped escorts within the treaty system.
segments.body: Clarke treats a carrier conversion or new carrier as a missed capability for the Mediterranean, even while recognizing the argument that land-based air power could cover the sea. He also criticizes the failure to pair available escort hulls with a sonar system that could be produced in sufficient numbers. The comparison emphasizes that technical availability matters only if procurement and production priorities turn it into fleet-wide capability.

evidence.note: Italian carrier possibilities and the limits of its escort sonar program are considered.

segments.title: South African sea-lane security at the Cape
segments.summary: The Cape's global shipping position made South African naval investment strategically valuable, but domestic politics narrowed the available choices.
segments.body: The lecture links South Africa's location to the protection of trade between the Atlantic and Indian Oceans, including the threat posed by raiders such as Graf Spee. Clarke argues that smaller ships could have improved local security and integration with wider Empire force structure. He also identifies recruitment and apartheid-era social constraints as part of why a larger naval commitment was politically difficult.

evidence.note: Cape geography, force requirements, and domestic constraints are examined.

segments.title: Why French parity with Italy was strategically awkward
segments.summary: France's equal treaty position with Italy did not match France's wider imperial commitments or its direct Mediterranean rivalry.
segments.body: Clarke's criticism is not focused on one French hull, but on the strategic consequences of accepting the same naval scale as Italy. France faced both Mediterranean competition and broad imperial commitments, while Italy's principal concerns were more concentrated. The comparison illustrates his wider point that equal treaty ratios could conceal unequal strategic burdens.

evidence.note: French-Italian parity is contrasted with their different strategic situations.

segments.title: Cruisers as presence ships and industrial investment
segments.summary: With cruiser tonnage initially unrestricted, regular construction could have supported imperial presence, shipyards, design expertise, and employment.
segments.body: The lecture stresses that Washington allowed as many cruisers as governments were prepared to fund beneath the displacement ceiling. Clarke argues that regular orders would have created both operational presence and a durable domestic industrial base. He frames naval spending as an economic choice with effects in shipyard employment, technical capacity, and regional development.

evidence.note: Unrestricted cruiser construction and its industrial effects are discussed.

segments.title: Indian sloops and vulnerable coastal logistics
segments.summary: More Indian-built sloops could have supported local industry while protecting a coastal transport system exposed during wartime.
segments.body: Clarke connects the Indian Navy's limited sloop force to the movement of food and goods by sea around the subcontinent. Once wartime threats disrupted that traffic, there were too few escorts and too few replacements available from a globally stretched British system. He also sees larger Indian naval construction as a way to develop industrial capability and visible national capacity.

evidence.note: India's cruiser and sloop possibilities are tied to domestic sea transport and wartime escort needs.

segments.title: Kaga conversion and Japan's treaty flexibility
segments.summary: The lecture questions Kaga's conversion after Amagi's loss and explores how Japan could have sought a more efficient carrier or hidden submarine growth.
segments.body: Clarke argues that a slower converted Kaga was a poor substitute for the fast carrier he believes naval aviation required, and suggests Japan could have asked to build a new carrier instead. He then turns to Japan's geography and control of access to its yards as advantages for concealing submarine construction. Both examples frame treaty limits as something states could exploit more imaginatively, or evade.

evidence.note: Amagi, Kaga, carrier speed, and Japanese submarine concealment are discussed.

segments.title: A Dominion sloop program as wartime mobilization insurance
segments.summary: Regular sloop orders across the Empire could have preserved escorts, trained workers, and shipyard capacity before the next war.
segments.body: Using a hypothetical annual construction program, Clarke shows how modest orders could accumulate into a large reserve of useful ships by 1939. The larger payoff is industrial: yards, turbine production, and skilled labor would already be prepared to expand into destroyers or other wartime requirements. The point is less the exact total than the value of a sustained building rhythm.

evidence.note: The hypothetical Empire-wide sloop program and its mobilization effects are explained.

segments.title: US cruiser shortages and the culture of scarce assets
segments.summary: A larger prewar cruiser force could have improved US naval presence, design development, and officers' experience of calculated risk.
segments.body: Clarke describes cruisers as the ships that make global presence possible when capital-ship construction is constrained. He also argues that scarcity can train commanders to protect valuable ships too carefully, while large destroyer forces more readily teach officers to judge necessary operational risk. The claim is institutional rather than a criticism of individual wartime commanders.

evidence.note: US cruiser construction, presence missions, and the effect of scarce assets on command culture are discussed.

segments.title: The missed Lexington battlecruiser option
segments.summary: Keeping two Lexingtons as battlecruisers could have given American carriers fast capital-ship escorts and a stronger scouting force.
segments.body: Clarke does not call the Colorado choice wrong; he instead contrasts a stronger battle line with the speed and reach the United States lacked in its scouting force. Modernized Lexington battlecruisers could have operated alongside carriers, supported risky strikes, and delayed stronger enemy ships long enough for a force to escape. The discussion uses Force H and Renown as an example of how fast capital ships can enable carrier operations.

evidence.note: The Colorado-Lexington trade-off and battlecruisers as carrier escorts are examined.

segments.title: Britain's missed escort and fast-capital-ship options
segments.summary: Britain's underfunded sloop program and the abandoned F3 design illustrate how repeated small savings could reduce future flexibility.
segments.body: Clarke criticizes interrupted Australian and British small-warship construction because lost yard capacity and escorts proved costly once war arrived. He then describes the F3 as a missed alternative to Nelson and Rodney: a fast ship with 15-inch/50-calibre guns could have influenced later modernization and German design calculations. The argument is about a different capability mix, not a claim that the Nelson class was a bad choice.

evidence.note: Australian and British sloop procurement leads into the F3 fast-battleship counterfactual.
